name: american black bean salad
instructions:
Make the ketchup vinaigrette per its own recipe.

Brown ground beef (or impossible meat / ground vegan meat) in a pan until well caramelized. Drain excess fat. Let cool slightly.

Build the salad: combine black beans (drained), finely chopped celery, finely diced red onions, chopped fresh dill, and the cooked-and-cooled ground meat. Toss with the ketchup vinaigrette.

Finish with shredded or torn soft cheese (monterrey jack or fontina) — let it melt slightly against the still-warm beef.
notes:
TARGET FLAVOR: a mcdonald's burger in salad form. Every ingredient maps to a component of that flavor profile:
- Black beans + ground beef: the patty.
- Ketchup vinaigrette: the ketchup + mustard + pickle relish flavor axis (the vinaigrette has ketchup, dijon, and macerated dill in it — see ketchup-vinaigrette recipe).
- Dill (extra, in the salad): amplifies the pickle note.
- Red onions: the classic burger onion.
- Soft cheese (monterrey jack or fontina): the melted cheese on the patty. Both melt into the warm beef and read as classic american burger cheese.
The whole thing is a deconstructed assembly that tastes recognizably mcdonald's-shaped without being a burger.

WHY SOFT CHEESE (NOT CHEDDAR): mcdonald's-style american cheese is a melting cheese with a mild flavor — monterrey jack and fontina are the closest analogs in the cookbook. Sharp cheddar would push the dish toward something different (smokier, more grown-up — less like the mcdonald's target).

MEAT — BEEF OR VEGAN: ground beef is the canonical choice for the burger target, but impossible meat and ground vegan meat both work and arguably read more burger-y than other vegan options (their soy-protein extrusion structure is specifically tuned to mimic beef). Brown the meat aggressively — the maillard sear is part of what makes this dish read as burger rather than as bean salad.

NO TOMATOES IN THE SALAD: the ketchup vinaigrette already carries the tomato note (concentrated, sweet, vinegar-balanced). Adding fresh tomato on top of that would be redundant and would push the dish away from the burger target toward a more generic mediterranean salad.

SERVE WARM, OR ROOM TEMP: the cheese needs the warm beef to melt into. Don't refrigerate before serving — chilling kills the cheese melt and the dish loses its burger-warm-bun character.
